lessons.title: What a Script Is: Shebang and chmod +x
lessons.content:
You already know how to type commands into the shell one at a time. But some jobs need the *same* handful of commands every time — backing up a folder, setting up a project, cleaning out old files. Retyping them is slow and easy to get wrong.

A **bash script** is the fix. A script is simply a plain text file that holds a list of shell commands. When you run the file, bash reads it top to bottom and runs each command in order, exactly as if you had typed them yourself. Think of it as **writing down a recipe** so you (or anyone) can repeat the steps perfectly, or a **macro** that replays your keystrokes.

Here is a complete tiny script:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "starting backup"
cp -r ~/work /backup
echo "done"
```

The first line, `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, is called the **shebang** (from "hash-bang", the `#!` characters). It tells the system *which program should run this file* — here, bash. Writing `#!/usr/bin/env bash` lets the system find bash wherever it is installed, which is the portable, works-everywhere form. The shebang must be the very first line.

Save that as `backup.sh`. One more step: a fresh text file is not yet allowed to run as a program. You give it permission once with **chmod +x** ("change mode, add e**x**ecutable"):

```bash
chmod +x backup.sh
./backup.sh
```

The `./` means "the file right here in this folder". From now on, `./backup.sh` replays all three commands.

**Recap:** a script is saved commands in a text file. Start it with the shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, make it runnable once with `chmod +x`, then run it with `./name.sh`.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a bash script?
choices: A special compiled program only experts can write
A plain text file containing shell commands that bash runs in order
A setting that changes how the terminal looks
A folder that stores your command history
items.explanation: A script is just a text file listing commands; running it is the same as typing those commands yourself, one after another. It is not compiled and it is not a terminal setting.

items.prompt: What is the main reason to put commands in a script instead of typing them each time?
choices: Scripts run faster than typing the same commands
So you can repeat a task exactly without retyping it, and share it
Typing commands by hand is not allowed on Linux
Scripts use less disk space than commands
items.explanation: A script captures a repeatable recipe: run it any time to redo the steps perfectly, and hand it to someone else. The commands themselves run at the same speed whether typed or scripted.

items.prompt: What does the shebang line `#!/usr/bin/env bash` do?
choices: It prints a welcome message when the script starts
It tells the system which program (bash) should run this file
It is a comment that bash ignores completely
It makes the file executable, replacing chmod
items.explanation: The shebang names the interpreter for the file, so the system knows to run it with bash. It does not print anything and does not set the executable permission — that is chmod's job.

items.prompt: Where must the shebang line appear in a script?
choices: Anywhere, as long as it is somewhere in the file
items.explanation: The shebang only works as the first line of the file. If anything (even a blank line) comes before it, the system will not recognize it as a shebang.

items.prompt: Why do you run `chmod +x backup.sh` before running the script?
choices: To save the file to disk
To give the file permission to run as a program
To translate the script into another language
To delete the old version of the file
items.explanation: A new text file is not executable by default. `chmod +x` adds the 'executable' permission so the system will let you run it. You only need to do this once per file.

items.prompt: You made the script executable. How do you run a script named backup.sh that is in your current folder?
items.explanation: `./backup.sh` runs the file in the current folder; the `./` means 'right here'. There is no `run` or `open` command for this, and `bash.exe` is a Windows-style name, not how you launch it on Linux.

items.prompt: In what order does bash run the lines of a script?
choices: In a random order each time
Top to bottom, one line after another
All lines at the same time
items.explanation: Bash reads the script from the top and runs each command in turn before moving to the next. This predictable top-to-bottom order is what makes a script behave like a recipe.

items.prompt: Inside a script, what does a line that starts with `#` (other than the shebang) do?
choices: It runs as a special command
It is a comment — a note for humans that bash ignores
items.explanation: A `#` begins a comment, so bash skips the rest of that line. Comments let you leave notes explaining what the script does. The shebang only has special meaning because of the `!` and its position on line 1.

items.prompt: Why is `#!/usr/bin/env bash` often preferred over hard-coding a fixed path to bash?
choices: It runs the script twice for safety
It lets the system find bash wherever it happens to be installed, so the script is more portable
It makes the script run without bash at all
It is required for comments to work
items.explanation: Using `env` looks bash up in the normal search path, so the script works even when bash lives in a different location on another machine. It does not run anything twice and bash is still required.

lessons.title: Variables and echo
lessons.content:
A script gets far more useful once it can *remember* things. A **variable** is a labelled box that holds a piece of text. You put a value in it once and reuse it by name, so you do not repeat yourself and you can change a value in one place.

You create a variable by writing `name=value`. The rule that trips up every beginner: **no spaces around the `=`**.

```bash
name="Sam"
greeting="Hello"
```

(`name = "Sam"` with spaces does NOT work — bash would think `name` is a command.)

To *use* the value later, put a `$` in front of the name. This is called **expansion**: bash replaces `$name` with the text inside the box.

The **echo** command simply prints text to the screen. Combine them:

```bash
name="Sam"
echo "Hello $name"        # prints: Hello Sam
```

Quotes matter here. **Double quotes** `"..."` still let `$name` turn into its value. **Single quotes** `'...'` are completely literal — they print the `$name` as-is:

```bash
echo "Hi $name"     # Hi Sam
echo 'Hi $name'     # Hi $name
```

A habit to start right now: always wrap a variable in double quotes, like `"$name"`. You will see exactly why in a later lesson (it protects values that contain spaces). You can also capture the output of a command into a variable with `$( )`:

```bash
today=$(date +%F)        # today holds something like 2026-06-19
echo "Report for $today"
```

**Recap:** set a variable with `name=value` (no spaces). Read it back as `$name`, ideally quoted as `"$name"`. `echo` prints text; double quotes expand variables, single quotes do not.

items.prompt: What is a variable in a bash script?
choices: A labelled place that holds a value you can reuse by name
items.explanation: A variable is a named box for a value (usually text). You store something once and refer back to it by name, which keeps scripts tidy and easy to change.

items.prompt: Which line correctly sets a variable named city to the value London?
items.explanation: Assignment is `name=value` with NO spaces around the `=`. `city = "London"` fails because bash reads `city` as a command. The `$` is only for reading the value back, not for setting it.

items.prompt: Why does `count = 5` (with spaces around =) fail in bash?
choices: Numbers cannot be stored in variables
Bash reads `count` as a command name and `= 5` as its arguments
The value must be in quotes
items.explanation: With spaces, bash treats the first word as a command to run. Assignment must be written tightly as `count=5`. (Variables can hold numbers, and capitals are not required.)

items.prompt: You set `fruit="apple"`. How do you use that value later?
items.explanation: Put a `$` in front of the name to expand it: `$fruit` becomes `apple`. Writing just `fruit` is plain text, and `&` / function-call syntax are not how bash reads variables.

items.prompt: What does the `echo` command do?
choices: It deletes text from a file
It prints text to the screen (standard output)
It repeats the last command you ran
items.explanation: `echo` writes its arguments to the screen. It is the simplest way to show a message or the value of a variable. It does not delete anything or replay commands.

items.prompt: Given `name="Sam"`, what does `echo "Hi $name"` print?
items.explanation: Inside double quotes, `$name` is expanded to its value, so the output is `Hi Sam`. Double quotes allow variable expansion while still grouping the text together.

items.prompt: Given `name="Sam"`, what does `echo 'Hi $name'` print (note the SINGLE quotes)?
items.explanation: Single quotes are fully literal: nothing inside them is expanded, so `$name` is printed exactly as typed. Use double quotes when you want the variable replaced by its value.

items.prompt: What does `today=$(date +%F)` do?
choices: Sets today to the literal text 'date +%F'
Runs the date command and stores its output in the variable today
Prints the date to the screen
items.explanation: `$( )` runs the command inside and captures its output as text. So `today` holds the result of `date +%F` (a date like 2026-06-19). This is called command substitution.

items.prompt: Which is the recommended habit when using a variable's value?
choices: Wrap it in double quotes, like "$file"
Wrap it in single quotes, like '$file'
items.explanation: Double-quoting `"$file"` is the safe default — it protects values that contain spaces (covered fully in a later lesson). Single quotes would stop the value from expanding at all, and capitalization does not affect safety.

lessons.title: Making Decisions: if, [[ ]] Tests, and case
lessons.content:
So far a script runs every line no matter what. A real program needs to **make decisions**: do this *only if* something is true. That is what `if` is for.

The shape is `if` ... `then` ... `fi` (`fi` is `if` spelled backwards, marking the end):

```bash
if [[ -f report.txt ]]; then
    echo "found it"
else
    echo "missing"
fi
```

The part in `[[ ... ]]` is the **test** — a question that is either true or false. The `then` block runs when the test is true; the optional `else` block runs otherwise. Always use the modern `[[ ]]` form in bash; the older `[ ]` (also spelled `test`) works but is fussier about spaces and quoting, so prefer `[[ ]]`.

Common tests:

```bash
[[ -f path ]]       # true if path is a file that exists
[[ -d path ]]       # true if path is a directory
[[ -z "$x" ]]       # true if the string $x is empty
[[ "$a" == "$b" ]]  # true if the two strings are equal
```

Comparing **numbers** uses a different bracket, the arithmetic `(( ))`:

```bash
if (( count > 0 )); then echo "have $count"; fi
```

Use `(( ))` for math (`>`, `<`, `==` on numbers) and `[[ ]]` for files and strings. (A classic mix-up: inside `[[ ]]`, `>` compares text alphabetically, not by size.)

When you have many possible values, a long `if`/`elif` chain gets clumsy. **case** is cleaner for matching one value against several options:

```bash
case "$1" in
  start) echo "starting" ;;
  stop)  echo "stopping" ;;
  *)     echo "usage: start|stop" ;;
esac
```

Each branch ends with `;;`, and `*)` is the catch-all default.

**Recap:** `if [[ test ]]; then ... fi` runs code only when the test is true; add `else` for the other path. Use `(( ))` for number comparisons, and `case` to match one value against many options.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of an `if` statement in a script?
choices: To repeat a command many times
To run a block of code only when a test is true
items.explanation: `if` lets the script branch: the `then` block runs only when the test is true. Repeating commands is what loops do, which is a separate idea.

items.prompt: What marks the end of an `if` block in bash?
items.explanation: Bash closes an `if` with `fi` ("if" reversed). It does not use `end`, curly braces, or `endif` for this. Similarly `case` ends with `esac`.

items.prompt: What does the test `[[ -f config.txt ]]` check?
choices: Whether config.txt exists and is a regular file
Whether you can write to config.txt
items.explanation: `-f` is true when the path exists and is a regular file. `-d` would test for a directory, and `-z` tests whether a string is empty.

items.prompt: In `if [[ -d backups ]]; then echo yes; else echo no; fi`, when does it print `no`?
choices: When `backups` is NOT an existing directory
items.explanation: The `else` block runs whenever the test is false — here, when `backups` is not an existing directory. If it is a directory, the `then` block prints `yes` instead.

items.prompt: Which test is true when the variable mode equals the text "on"?
items.explanation: String comparison uses `==` (or `=`) inside `[[ ]]`, with the variable quoted: `[[ "$mode" == "on" ]]`. `(( ))` is for numbers, not text, and there is no `equals` keyword.

items.prompt: Which is the right way to check whether the number in `count` is greater than 0?
items.explanation: `(( count > 0 ))` does a numeric comparison. Inside `[[ ]]`, `>` compares strings alphabetically, so `[[ 9 > 10 ]]` would be true — a classic bug. Use `(( ))` for math.

items.prompt: Why is `[[ ]]` preferred over the older `[ ]` for tests in a bash script?
choices: `[[ ]]` is the only one that works at all
`[[ ]]` is more forgiving — it handles unquoted variables and empty values more safely
items.explanation: `[[ ]]` is a bash keyword that avoids many word-splitting and quoting pitfalls of the older `[ ]` command. `[ ]` still works (and is needed for strict POSIX sh), but `[[ ]]` is safer in bash.

items.prompt: When is `case` a better choice than a long `if`/`elif` chain?
choices: When you need to loop over files
When you are matching one value against several possible options
When you only have a single true/false test
When you want to do arithmetic
items.explanation: `case` cleanly dispatches one value (like a command word) to many branches, each ending in `;;`. For a single condition, a plain `if` is simpler; for math you use `(( ))`.

items.prompt: In a `case` statement, what does the `*)` branch do?
choices: It is the catch-all default that matches anything not listed above
items.explanation: `*)` matches any value that did not match an earlier pattern, so it acts as the default (often an error/usage message). It has nothing to do with multiplication.

lessons.title: Loops: for and while
lessons.content:
Computers are great at doing the same thing many times. A **loop** repeats a block of commands so you do not have to copy-paste it. Bash has two everyday loops: `for` and `while`.

A **for loop** runs its body once for each item in a list. The body sits between `do` and `done`:

```bash
for color in red green blue; do
    echo "$color"
done
```

On each pass, the variable (`color` here) is set to the next item. So this prints `red`, then `green`, then `blue`. The list can be words you type, a number range, or a glob of filenames:

```bash
for n in 1 2 3; do echo "$n"; done       # 1 2 3
for f in *.txt; do echo "$f"; done        # every .txt file in this folder
```

Using a glob like `*.txt` directly is the **safe** way to loop over files, because bash hands each real filename to the loop intact — even names with spaces.

A **while loop** repeats *as long as a test stays true*. It is the right tool when you do not know the count ahead of time:

```bash
count=1
while (( count <= 3 )); do
    echo "pass $count"
    (( count++ ))         # add 1 to count
done
```

If you forget to change the test (here, increasing `count`), the loop never ends — an **infinite loop**. Press Ctrl-C to stop a runaway loop.

A very common `while` pattern is reading a file one line at a time, which you will meet again later:

```bash
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "got: $line"
done < notes.txt
```

**Recap:** a `for` loop repeats once per item in a list (use `*.txt` to loop over files safely); a `while` loop repeats while a test is true (remember to change the test, or it runs forever).

items.prompt: What does a loop do in a script?
choices: Runs a block of commands repeatedly
items.explanation: A loop repeats a block of commands so you avoid copy-pasting. `for` repeats per item in a list; `while` repeats while a condition holds.

items.prompt: How many times does the body run in `for x in a b c d; do echo "$x"; done`?
items.explanation: A `for` loop runs once per item in the list. The list `a b c d` has four items, so the body runs four times, printing each in turn.

items.prompt: Which keywords wrap the body of a bash loop?
items.explanation: Both `for` and `while` loops put their body between `do` and `done`. `then ... fi` belongs to `if`, and bash loops do not use `begin/end` or braces for this.

items.prompt: In `for color in red green blue; do echo "$color"; done`, what is `$color` during the second pass?
choices: the whole list red green blue
items.explanation: Each pass sets the loop variable to the next item. Pass one is `red`, pass two is `green`, pass three is `blue`. The variable holds one item at a time, not the whole list.

items.prompt: What does `for f in *.txt; do ... done` loop over?
choices: Each file ending in .txt in the current folder
Every file on the whole system
items.explanation: Bash expands the glob `*.txt` into the matching filenames in the current folder, and the loop runs once per file. This is the safe, recommended way to iterate over files.

items.prompt: A `while` loop keeps running...
choices: as long as its test stays true
items.explanation: A `while` loop checks its test before each pass and continues while the test is true. It is ideal when you do not know in advance how many passes you need.

items.prompt: What is an infinite loop, and how is it usually caused?
choices: A loop that runs zero times because the list is empty
A loop that never ends because its test never becomes false (e.g. you forgot to update the counter)
A loop that runs on another computer
items.explanation: If the condition never turns false — often because the counter is never increased — the loop runs forever. Press Ctrl-C to stop it. Make sure each pass moves toward ending the loop.

items.prompt: What does `while IFS= read -r line; do ...; done < notes.txt` do?
choices: Reads notes.txt one line at a time into the variable line
items.explanation: This is the standard pattern for reading a file line by line: each pass puts the next line into `line`. The `< notes.txt` feeds the file in as input. `read -r` keeps the line exactly as written.

items.prompt: A loop is running forever and flooding your screen. What stops it?
choices: Waiting; it will end on its own
items.explanation: Ctrl-C sends an interrupt that stops the running command. An infinite loop will not end on its own, and there is no magic `stop` word for it.

lessons.title: Reading Arguments: $1, $@, shift, getopts
lessons.content:
A script becomes a real reusable tool when you can feed it different inputs each time, instead of hard-coding values. The words you type *after* the script name are its **arguments**, and the script reads them through special variables.

If you run:

```bash
./greet.sh Sam Alex Jo
```

then inside the script:

- `$1` is the first argument (`Sam`), `$2` the second (`Alex`), `$3` the third (`Jo`).
- `$@` is *all* the arguments as a list — always write it quoted, `"$@"`, so each one stays separate even if it contains spaces.
- `$#` is the *count* of arguments (here, `3`). `$0` is the script's own name.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "first is $1"
echo "there are $# arguments"
for name in "$@"; do
    echo "hello $name"
done
```

It is good manners to check you actually got an argument before using it:

```bash
if [[ -z "$1" ]]; then
    echo "usage: greet.sh NAME" >&2
    exit 1
fi
```

**shift** drops `$1` and slides everything down: the old `$2` becomes the new `$1`. It is handy for processing arguments one at a time in a loop.

For proper *named options* like `-o output.txt` or `-v`, counting positions gets awkward. Bash provides **getopts** to parse single-letter flags cleanly:

```bash
while getopts "o:v" opt; do
  case "$opt" in
    o) out="$OPTARG" ;;   # the colon after o means it takes a value
    v) verbose=1 ;;
    *) echo "usage: ..." >&2; exit 2 ;;
  esac
done
```

**Recap:** arguments after the script name arrive as `$1`, `$2`, ...; `"$@"` is the whole list and `$#` is the count. Use `shift` to step through them, and `getopts` for named `-x` style options.

items.prompt: What are a script's 'arguments'?
choices: The words you type after the script name when you run it
items.explanation: Arguments are the extra words supplied on the command line after the script name, like the file or name you want it to act on. They let one script handle different inputs.

items.prompt: You run `./backup.sh photos`. Inside the script, what is `$1`?
items.explanation: `$1` is the first argument after the script name, which is `photos`. The script's own name is `$0`, not `$1`.

items.prompt: Which variable holds ALL the arguments as a list?
items.explanation: `$@` is the full list of arguments (best written quoted as `"$@"`). `$#` is just the count, `$0` is the script name, and `$?` is the exit status of the last command.

items.prompt: You run `./tool.sh a b c`. What is the value of `$#`?
items.explanation: `$#` is the number of arguments passed, which is 3 here. To see the arguments themselves you would use `"$@"`.

items.prompt: Why should you usually write `"$@"` with quotes when looping over arguments?
choices: So each argument stays as one piece, even if it contains spaces
Quotes are required or bash crashes
items.explanation: Quoted `"$@"` keeps every argument intact as a separate word, so an argument like `my file.txt` is not split on its space. Unquoted, it would break apart — the same quoting lesson as for any variable.

items.prompt: What does the `shift` command do to a script's arguments?
choices: Drops $1 so that the old $2 becomes the new $1
Reverses the order of all arguments
Adds a new argument at the end
items.explanation: `shift` removes the first argument and slides the rest down by one position. It is useful for processing arguments one at a time in a loop.

items.prompt: Why check `if [[ -z "$1" ]]; then ...; exit 1; fi` near the top of a script?
choices: To make the script run twice
To stop with a helpful message when the required argument is missing
items.explanation: `-z "$1"` is true when the first argument is empty (not supplied). Catching that lets you print a usage message and exit instead of failing in a confusing way later.

items.prompt: When is `getopts` the right tool?
choices: When you need to parse named single-letter options like -o and -v
When you want to loop over files
When you need to do arithmetic
When you want to print text
items.explanation: `getopts` cleanly parses short options such as `-v` or `-o value`, rather than you counting positional arguments by hand. For plain inputs, `$1`/`$@` are enough.

items.prompt: In a getopts string like `"o:v"`, what does the colon after `o` mean?
choices: The -o option takes a value (an argument), available in $OPTARG
It marks the end of the options
items.explanation: A trailing colon means that option expects a value; getopts puts that value in `$OPTARG`. So `o:` means `-o something`, while `v` (no colon) is a simple on/off flag.

lessons.title: Robust Scripts: Quoting, set -euo pipefail, Functions, Cleanup, and When to Use Python
lessons.content:
A script that works on your machine today should not quietly break tomorrow. This lesson collects the habits that make scripts trustworthy.

**Always quote your variables.** This is the single most common bash bug. If a filename has a space, an unquoted variable splits into two words:

```bash
file="my report.txt"
rm $file        # WRONG: bash sees rm "my" "report.txt" — two names!
rm "$file"      # RIGHT: stays one name
```

The same trap is why `for f in $(ls)` is buggy: the output of `ls` is split on spaces, so a name like `my report.txt` becomes two items. Loop with a glob (`for f in *`) and quote `"$f"` instead.

**Fail fast with `set -euo pipefail`.** Put this right under the shebang:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
```

- `-e`: stop on the first command that fails, instead of charging on.
- `-u`: error if you use a variable you never set (catches typos like `$flie`).
- `-o pipefail`: a pipeline fails if *any* stage fails, not just the last.

**Exit codes** are how programs report success: `0` means success, any other number means an error. Your script returns one with `exit 0` (good) or `exit 1` (problem); the special variable `$?` holds the last command's code.

**Functions** group commands under a name so you can reuse them. Mark a function's own variables `local` so they do not leak out:

```bash
greet() {
    local who="$1"
    echo "hello $who"
}
greet Sam
```

**Clean up with `trap`.** A `trap` runs a command when the script exits — even if it fails partway — so temporary files always get removed:

```bash
tmp=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$tmp"' EXIT
```

**Debugging:** run `bash -x script.sh` to see each command as it executes, and run **ShellCheck** (`shellcheck script.sh`), a free tool that flags quoting and other common mistakes before they bite.

**When to switch to Python.** Bash is perfect for short scripts that glue commands together. But once a script grows past roughly 100 lines, needs real data structures (lists, dictionaries, JSON), makes web/API calls, or you want to write tests, reach for **Python** (`subprocess`, `pathlib`, `argparse`) instead — it stays readable where bash gets painful.

**Recap:** quote every `"$var"`, start with `set -euo pipefail`, return clear exit codes, group reuse into `local` functions, clean up with `trap ... EXIT`, lint with ShellCheck — and move to Python when the job outgrows simple glue.

items.prompt: Why should you write `rm "$file"` instead of `rm $file`?
choices: Without quotes, a filename containing a space splits into two separate names
rm requires quotes or it errors
Quotes delete the file more thoroughly
items.explanation: An unquoted `$file` is split on spaces, so `my report.txt` becomes two arguments (`my` and `report.txt`) and rm acts on the wrong things. Quoting keeps the value as a single piece. This is the most common bash bug.

items.prompt: Why is `for f in $(ls)` considered buggy?
choices: ls is not a real command
The output of ls is split on spaces, so filenames containing spaces break into multiple items
It only ever lists one file
items.explanation: The unquoted command output is word-split, so a name like `my report.txt` is treated as two entries. The safe pattern is `for f in *` (a glob), with the body using `"$f"`.

items.prompt: What does the `-e` in `set -euo pipefail` do?
choices: Makes the script stop as soon as a command fails
items.explanation: `-e` (errexit) halts the script on the first command that returns a failure, so it does not keep running after something has already gone wrong.

items.prompt: What problem does the `-u` option catch?
choices: Using a variable that was never set (often a typo in its name)
items.explanation: `-u` (nounset) makes referencing an unset variable an error, so a misspelling like `$flie` is caught immediately instead of silently expanding to nothing.

items.prompt: By convention, what exit code does a program return to mean 'success'?
items.explanation: An exit code of 0 means success; any non-zero value signals an error. Your script reports this with `exit 0` or `exit 1`, and `$?` holds the last command's code.

items.prompt: Why declare a function's variables with `local`?
choices: So they stay inside the function and do not overwrite variables outside it
It is required or the function will not run
items.explanation: Bash variables are global by default, so an unmarked assignment inside a function can clobber a variable elsewhere. `local` keeps the function's variables private to it, avoiding surprising bugs.

items.prompt: What is the benefit of `trap 'rm -f "$tmp"' EXIT`?
choices: It deletes the script after running
It removes the temp file whenever the script exits, even if it fails partway
It traps and ignores all errors
It runs the script on every login
items.explanation: A `trap ... EXIT` runs your cleanup on every exit path — normal finish, error, or interruption — so a temporary file is always cleaned up. A plain `rm` at the bottom would be skipped if the script exited early.

items.prompt: How can you watch each command run, with its values filled in, to debug a script?
items.explanation: `bash -x script.sh` traces execution, printing each command after expansion just before it runs — great for seeing where things go wrong. ShellCheck is a separate, static check that does not run the script.

items.prompt: What is ShellCheck?
choices: A command that runs your script as root
A free tool that reads your script and warns about common mistakes like missing quotes
items.explanation: ShellCheck statically analyzes a script and flags issues such as unquoted variables and other common bugs, before you ever run it. Running it and fixing its findings is a great habit.

items.prompt: Which situation is the strongest sign you should switch from bash to Python?
choices: The script uses a single pipe
The script needs to parse and manipulate nested JSON / data structures with real error handling
The script has one for loop
items.explanation: Nested data, JSON/HTTP, and real error handling are exactly where bash gets painful and Python (with json, pathlib, try/except, tests) shines. A single pipe, one loop, or calling grep are everyday bash and not reasons to switch.

items.prompt: What is bash best suited for, compared with Python?
choices: Large programs with complex data structures and unit tests
Short scripts that glue command-line tools and pipelines together
items.explanation: Bash excels at short glue: chaining commands, redirection, and file work. Once you need data structures, tests, or lots of logic (roughly past ~100 lines), Python is the clearer choice.
